title: A global analysis of the impact of COVID-19 stay-at-home restrictions on crime
data_sources: Police-recorded daily crime incident counts (27 cities, 23 countries)
Oxford COVID-19 Government Response Tracker (stay-at-home stringency sub-index)
NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI)
core_conclusion: COVID-19 stay-at-home restrictions caused a mean 37% drop in urban crime across 27 cities in 23 countries, with property crimes (robbery −46%, theft −47%) falling far more steeply than homicide (−14%). More stringent restrictions on public-space movement predicted larger declines, consistent with routine activity and opportunity theory rather than psychological strain as the dominant mechanism.
why_it_matters: First global cross-national natural experiment in criminology; settles the debate between opportunity theory and strain theory in favor of the former; quantifies a dose-response relationship between policy stringency and crime decline (~35% of variance explained for property crimes); reveals the homicide exception pointing to organized-crime and domestic contexts as mobility-independent crime domains; provides a causal foundation for mobility-sensitive urban safety policy.
why_nature: Planetary-scale natural experiment on human behavioral response to policy; rigorous ITS + meta-analytic causal design; cross-disciplinary integration of criminology, behavioral economics, epidemiology and policy; dose-response nuance rather than blanket headline; open data deposit meeting Nature reproducibility standards.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatiotemporally disaggregating the crime-mobility link with MGWR/GWR for neighborhood-level causal inference
Physics-informed GNN trained on mobility-crime co-evolution across cities for transferable crime prediction
Multi-source data fusion (hotline, ED visits, cybercrime) to estimate the public-to-indoor/online crime displacement budget
Causal ML (causal forests + SHAP) to identify which spatial features moderate equity effects of lockdown crime reductions across neighborhoods
2SFCA-based public-safety accessibility metrics calibrated against the paper's empirically validated mobility-crime relationship

related.title: COVID-19 is linked to changes in the time–space dimension of human mobility
related.relation: quantifies the spatiotemporal restructuring of mobility during COVID-19 — the behavioral mechanism underlying this paper's crime declines

related.title: Mobility network models of COVID-19 explain inequities and inform reopening
related.relation: bipartite mobility network model linking movement to outcomes — methodological analogue for extending this paper's mobility-crime framework

related.title: Behavioral changes during the COVID-19 pandemic decreased income diversity of urban encounters
related.relation: COVID-induced mobility changes restructured who meets whom in cities — directly relevant to the opportunity/encounter mechanisms in this paper

related.title: Socio-spatial inequality and the effects of density on COVID-19 transmission in US cities
related.relation: neighborhood-level socioeconomic heterogeneity in COVID policy impacts — mirrors this paper's call for sub-city equity analyses

related.title: Urban scaling of firearm violence, ownership and accessibility in the United States
related.relation: urban scaling perspective on violence — complements this paper's cross-city comparison by providing a scaling-law baseline for expected crime levels

related.title: Reduction in mobility and COVID-19 transmission
related.relation: establishes the mobility-transmission quantitative relationship used as validation parallel for the mobility-crime relationship

figures.caption: Seven-day moving average time series of daily crime counts (indexed to 100 at restriction start) for assault (n=23), burglary (n=20), robbery (n=24), theft (n=16), vehicle theft (n=20), and homicide (n=25) across cities
Forest plot of IRRs and 95% CIs for stay-at-home restrictions on daily violent crimes (assault, robbery, homicide) with random-effects meta-analytic summary effects
Forest plot of IRRs and 95% CIs for stay-at-home restrictions on daily property crimes (burglary, theft, vehicle theft) with random-effects meta-analytic summary effects
Scatterplot of average crime decline by city vs. stay-at-home stringency index (n=27 cities), with fitted linear dose-response relationship and 95% CI
